topic: A person discovers their partner has been taking secret phone calls
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had curdled into something thick and suffocating. I found the evidence tucked under a loose floorboard—a burner phone, the kind you only see in spy movies, and a stack of recent call logs that made my stomach clench. When I finally confronted him, Mark, his nonchalant posture stiffened instantly. He didn't deny it. Instead, he looked at me with a kind of weary resignation, as if I were the one overreacting to a minor inconvenience. "It's nothing," he mumbled, picking at a loose thread on his sweater. My jaw tightened. I didn't raise my voice. I just stood there, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white, staring at the floor until I was sure he understood the gravity of what I was holding. I turned and walked to the kitchen, pouring myself a glass of water, the ice clinking too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 2]

I watched him walk in, the sound of his key turning in the lock echoing the sudden, sharp clarity in my mind. The phone was sitting on the bedside table, glowing faintly. I didn't shout. I didn't even yell. I just picked it up, my fingers tracing the screen as I scrolled through the unfamiliar numbers. A slow, cold burn started low in my chest, spreading outward until my hands trembled slightly. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of startled annoyance, like I’d just interrupted a very important, very private transaction. "What are you doing?" he asked, his tone sharp. I dropped the phone onto the duvet, the sound barely audible. I didn't meet his eyes. I simply pulled the covers higher around myself, a deliberate physical withdrawal, refusing to give him the satisfaction of seeing the tremor running through me.

[story 3]

It took three days of careful observation before the pieces clicked into place. The subtle shifts in his routine, the way he became instantly unreachable when he took a call, the furtive glances toward the door. Then, I found the discarded receipt for a prepaid SIM card. When I finally cornered him in the living room, the air crackled with unspoken accusation. He tried to play it off as a misunderstanding, a silly overreaction on my part. But I felt a strange, solid core of refusal settle within me. I leaned against the doorframe, crossing my arms over my chest, letting the stillness of my stance speak volumes. "I know," I stated, my voice level and surprisingly steady. "And I’m not going to let this change how I see things."
